en.extract:
The crested pigeon (Ocyphaps lophotes) is a bird found widely throughout mainland Australia except for the far northern tropical areas. Only two Australian pigeon species possess an erect crest, the crested pigeon and the spinifex pigeon. The crested pigeon is the larger of the two species. The crested pigeon is sometimes referred to as a topknot pigeon, a common name shared with the reddish crested Lopholaimus antarcticus of Eastern Australia.


Taxonomy
The species was described in 1822 by Temminck; it is the only species in the genus Ocyphaps, established by G.R. Gray in 1842. Two subspecies are recognized: O. l. lophotes, the nominate subspecies, occurring across the south of the continent; and O. l. whitlocki, occurring in regions of the centre and north of Western Australia. The taxon was named for F. Lawson Whitlock, who made important collections of bird specimens in those areas.
The species has also been placed as Geophaps, perhaps allied with Geophaps plumifera.
Common names for the species, aside from crested or topknot pigeon, characterise them as a bronzewing or dove that is likewise distinguished by the crest.


Description

The length of the crested pigeon varies from 30 to 34 cm (12 to 13 in). Colouration is grey with tinges of brown, pink and green. It has a feathered, but slender, black spike on top of the head. It runs with the crest erect. The periorbital skin is bright orange. Wings have black stripes and are bronzed, while the primary feathers have iridescent areas of brown, purple, blue, and green.  Immature birds have duller colours with no bronzing on the wings. The birds can be normally seen in two sizes, they can be thin or plumped. It is almost impossible to tell if a crested pigeon is male or female, because they have the same plumage.
If startled, the crested pigeon takes to the air with a distinctive whistling 'call', the source of the noise can be attributed to the way the air rushes over a modified primary feather found on the wings.


Distribution and habitat
The crested pigeon is found in lightly wooded grasslands in both rural and urban areas, but they can also be seen at watercourses, homestead gardens, pastoral areas, sports grounds, and golf courses. Their habitat has expanded rapidly since settlement has produced pastoral lands (previously they were only found in inland and Western Australia). Foraging for grains, they have adapted to grain farming areas, often feeding on the noxious weed of Echium plantagineum.


Behaviour

Their most distinctive behaviour is the beating and whistling sound their wings make when they take off. This is most likely to draw the attention of predators to birds on the wing, and away from any birds remaining on the ground, and as an alarm call to other pigeons.  When the birds land, their tails tilt upwards and the flight patterns are similar to those of the spotted turtle dove. They can be highly social and tend to be seen in flocks of varying sizes. They are highly gregarious birds when in contact with humans.


Breeding

While they breed throughout the year, it is more common in the warmer months. Males approach females and begin an elaborate mating dance; they bob their bodies up and down, while opening and closing their wings like a fan with each bob. This is accompanied by a soft hooting which is timed with the bobbing. If the female is interested, she remains generally stationary as the male approaches, until copulation is attempted. Nesting usually occurs in shrubs or trees. Nests usually consist of a platform of twigs. They lay two oval, white, glossy eggs. The eggs usually hatch 3 weeks after they were laid. Both parents incubate the eggs.


References

Pizzey and Knight, "Field Guide to the Birds of Australia", Angus & Robertson, ISBN 0-207-19691-5
Trounsen and Trounsen, "Australian Birds: A Concise Photographic Field Guide, Cameron House. ISBN 1-875999-47-7.


External links

Birds in Backyards

The '''crested pigeon''' ('''''Ocyphaps lophotes''''') is a [[bird]] found widely throughout mainland [[Australia]] except for the far northern [[tropical]] areas. Only two Australian pigeon species possess an erect crest, the crested pigeon and the [[spinifex pigeon]]. The crested pigeon is the larger of the two species. The crested pigeon is sometimes referred to as a '''topknot pigeon''', a common name shared with the reddish crested ''[[Lopholaimus antarcticus]]'' of Eastern Australia.

== Taxonomy ==
The species was described in 1822 by [[Coenraad Jacob Temminck|Temminck]]; it is the only species in the genus ''Ocyphaps'', established by [[George Robert Gray|G.R. Gray]] in 1842. Two subspecies are recognized: ''O. l. lophotes'', the [[Nominate (taxonomy)|nominate]] subspecies, occurring across the south of the continent; and ''O. l. whitlocki'', occurring in regions of the centre and north of Western Australia. The taxon was named for [[F. Lawson Whitlock]], who made important collections of bird specimens in those areas.

The species has also been placed as ''[[Geophaps]]'', perhaps allied with ''[[Geophaps plumifera]]''.<ref name="hbw">Baptista, L.F., Trail, P.W., Horblit, H.M. & Boesman, P. (2018). Crested Pigeon (Ocyphaps lophotes). In: del Hoyo, J., Elliott, A., Sargatal, J., Christie, D.A. & de Juana, E. (eds.). Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive. Lynx Edicions, Barcelona. (retrieved from https://www.hbw.com/node/54193 on 21 July 2018).</ref>
Common names for the species, aside from crested or topknot pigeon, characterise them as a [[Bronzewing pigeon|bronzewing]] or [[dove]] that is likewise distinguished by the crest.<ref name="birdfinder">{{cite book |last1=Morcombe |first1=Michael |title=The great Australian birdfinder |date=1986 |publisher=Lansdowne Press |location=Sydney |isbn=0-7018-1962-6 |page=320}}</ref>

==Description==
[[File:Crested Pigeon Nov09.jpg|thumb|left]]
[[File:Pigeon-Crested.jpg|thumb]]
The length of the crested pigeon varies from {{convert|30|to|34|cm|in|abbr=on}}. Colouration is grey with tinges of brown, pink and green. It has a feathered, but slender, black spike on top of the head. It runs with the crest erect. The periorbital skin is bright orange. Wings have black stripes and are bronzed, while the primary feathers have iridescent areas of brown, purple, blue, and green.  [[Juvenile (organism)|Immature]] birds have duller colours with no bronzing on the wings. The birds can be normally seen in two sizes, they can be thin or plumped. It is almost impossible to tell if a crested pigeon is male or female, because they have the same plumage.

If startled, the crested pigeon takes to the air with a distinctive whistling 'call', the source of the noise can be attributed to the way the air rushes over a modified primary feather found on the wings.<ref>{{cite web |last=BirdLife Australia |title=Crested Pigeon {{!}} BirdLife Australia |url=http://birdlife.org.au/bird-profiles/Crested-Pigeon |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230923024528/https://birdlife.org.au/bird-profiles/crested-pigeon/ |archive-date=23 September 2023 |access-date=23 September 2023}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|author1=Ed Yong|author-link=Ed Yong|title=Sound the alarm – crested pigeons give off warning whistles simply by taking off – Phenomena|url=http://phenomena.nationalgeographic.com/2009/09/02/sound-the-alarm-crested-pigeons-give-off-warning-whistles-simply-by-taking-off/|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130123013754/http://phenomena.nationalgeographic.com/2009/09/02/sound-the-alarm-crested-pigeons-give-off-warning-whistles-simply-by-taking-off/|archive-date=January 23, 2013|website=[[National Geographic (magazine)|National Geographic]]|access-date=5 November 2015|date=2009-09-02}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
The crested pigeon is found in lightly wooded grasslands in both rural and urban areas, but they can also be seen at watercourses, homestead gardens, [[pastoral]] areas, [[sports ground]]s, and [[golf]] courses. Their habitat has expanded rapidly since settlement has produced pastoral lands (previously they were only found in inland and [[Western Australia]]).<ref>{{cite journal|last=Black|first=A.|year=2015|title=Range expansion of the Crested Pigeon, ''Ocyphaps lophotes'', in South Australia|journal=South Australian Ornithologist|volume=40|issue=2|pages=45–61}}</ref> Foraging for grains, they have adapted to grain farming areas, often feeding on the noxious weed of ''[[Echium plantagineum]]''.

==Behaviour==
[[File:Crested Pigeon94.ogg|thumbtime=33|200px|right|SE Queensland, Australia]]
Their most distinctive behaviour is the beating and [[whistle|whistling]] sound their wings make when they take off. This is most likely to draw the attention of [[predator]]s to birds on the wing, and away from any birds remaining on the ground, and as an alarm call to other pigeons.<ref>{{cite news |last=Gill |first=Victoria |date=2 September 2009 |title=Pigeons' wings sound the alarm |publisher=BBC News |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/science/nature/8232570.stm |access-date=2 September 2009}}</ref>  When the birds land, their tails tilt upwards and the flight patterns are similar to those of the [[spotted dove|spotted turtle dove]]. They can be highly social and tend to be seen in flocks of varying sizes. They are highly gregarious birds when in contact with humans.

===Breeding===
[[File:Crested Pigeon (Ocyphaps lophotes) mating display.jpg|thumb|Mating display]]
While they breed throughout the year, it is more common in the warmer months. Males approach females and begin an elaborate mating dance; they bob their bodies up and down, while opening and closing their wings like a fan with each bob. This is accompanied by a soft hooting which is timed with the bobbing. If the female is interested, she remains generally stationary as the male approaches, until copulation is attempted. [[Bird nest|Nest]]ing usually occurs in [[shrub]]s or [[tree]]s. Nests usually consist of a platform of twigs. They lay two oval, white, glossy [[egg (biology)|egg]]s. The eggs usually hatch 3 weeks after they were laid. Both parents incubate the eggs.

== References ==
{{Reflist}}
* Pizzey and Knight, "Field Guide to the Birds of Australia", Angus & Robertson, {{ISBN|0-207-19691-5}}
* Trounsen and Trounsen, "Australian Birds: A Concise Photographic Field Guide, Cameron House. {{ISBN|1-875999-47-7}}.

zh.extract:
冠鸠（学名：Ocyphaps lophotes）是冠鸠属的唯一物种，属于鸽形目鸠鸽科，是一种生活在澳洲大陆的鸟。除澳大利亚北部靠赤道地区外它在整个澳大利亚生活，长30至34厘米。


分布
这种鸟栖息在草地、灌木丛和林地中海，但在河边、园子、牧场、运动场和高尔夫球场上也可以看到它。由于人的活动，在澳大利亚建立牧场等，使得它的栖息地变广（一开始它只生活在澳大利亚西部）。由于这种鸟食草籽，它们尤其喜欢生活在粮田附近。


形态
冠鸠的羽毛总体来说是带有棕色的灰色。它的头顶上有一簇黑色的羽冠。在冠鳩奔跑时这簇羽冠直立。眼睛周围有一圈鲜艳的、橙色的环。翅膀上是青铜色，带有黑色的条纹。冠鸠的少数羽毛带有鲜艳的棕色、紫色、蓝色和绿色。幼鸟的羽毛颜色比较暗淡，翅膀上没有青铜色。
冠鸠會发重复的“whoop”的叫声，假如单声的话是警报声。


习性
冠鸠最特殊的是在它们起飞时的拍打和啸声。这个声音可以用来将敌人引向起飞的鸟，而让还在地上的鸟有足够的时间逃脱。降落时它们的尾巴翘起，与珠颈斑鸠类似。冠鸠一般成群活動，偶尔只有两只成对出现。与人有接触的冠鸠不怕人。


繁殖
冠鸠可以全年孵卵，不过一般更喜欢在温暖的月份里孵卵。巢一般筑在灌木或树上，一般是由树枝造成。每次产两只椭圆体的、白色的、光泽的蛋。


参考文献

冠鸠的图片 （页面存档备份，存于互联网档案馆）
